title: Pet Kennel End Table
summary: An end table that's a dog kennel — four wall panels with 1x2 rail slats, built and finished FLAT, then assembled; corner brackets make it a flat-packing crate.
description:
This is the end-table-sized sibling of the big dog-crate console: a single kennel with slatted 1x2 rail sides, a latched rail door, and a project-panel top that holds the lamp and the remote. Sized for a small-to-medium dog who wants a den, not a cage.

The construction insight is in step 9, stated as advice and worth treating as law: you are really building FOUR WALLS, and everything is easier flat. Each side and end builds as its own pocket-holed panel; the door gets its hinges and latch installed while the end wall lies on the bench (the source did, and called it much easier — they were right). Even finishing happens per-wall: filling pocket holes, sanding, painting a flat panel beats painting inside a box through a 17" door.

The flat-pack option is the sleeper feature: assemble the four walls with corner brackets screwed from the inside instead of glue, and the crate breaks down flat for travel or storage — a kennel that goes to the cabin in the trunk instead of staying home.

Rail spacing note: the 6" rails with 1x2s produce gaps fine for most dogs. For a determined puppy, add more rails per bay. Bottom is optional (housebroken dogs, as the source notes) — if you add the 1/4" plywood floor, the plexiglass lining trick makes it accident-proof. And a dog inside means ventilation is a feature, never a compromise: don't be tempted to panel over the rails for looks.

tools.note: 3/4" setting, 1-1/4" screws — every rail and frame joint.
Pilot holes for hinges and latch in 3/4" stock.

materials.name: Pine project panel, 24" x 36"
materials.note: The top. 3/4" plywood or MDF also works.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Side panels; leftover makes the optional floor.

materials.name: Latch
materials.note: Mounted where hands reach and paws don't.

materials.name: Corner brackets
materials.note: The flat-pack option — walls screw together from inside instead of gluing.

cutList.note: Batch-cut with a stop block — 21 identical parts is jig territory.

steps.title: Batch the rails, build the railing runs
steps.body:
Cut all 21 six-inch rails with a stop block so they're identical, drill a 3/4" pocket hole in each end, and assemble the slatted railing runs between the 1x3 side boards with 1-1/4" screws and glue.

Even spacing matters here for looks AND function — lay the rails out with a spacer scrap instead of measuring each gap.

steps.title: Complete the two side walls
steps.body: Add the 1x2 legs to each side assembly — pocket screws and glue, everything flush at the top where the panel meets the top later. Then brad-nail the 1/4" plywood to the lower portion of each side with 3/4" brads and glue.

steps.title: Build the back end wall
steps.body: Same recipe at end-wall width: 1x3 legs, back boards, plywood panel. You now have three of the four walls.

steps.title: Door and door wall — hardware while it's flat
steps.body:
Pocket-screw the door together (1x2 sides and rails in a 1x3 frame), then build the front wall's door frame. Now, with the front wall FLAT on the bench, install the hinges and the latch — pilot-drilled. The source did hardware at this stage and called it much easier; inside-a-box hinge mounting is nobody's favorite hour.

Check the door swings with an even gap before moving on.

steps.title: Finish flat, then assemble
steps.body:
Fill the pocket holes (the source filled three times — filler shrinks), sand to 120, and paint each wall as a flat panel.

Then assemble the four walls: pocket screws and glue for permanent, or corner brackets screwed from the inside for a crate that flat-packs for travel. The brackets cost a few dollars and buy a feature; take that trade.

steps.title: Top on, floor optional
steps.body:
Screw the project panel to the top from below. For a floor, cut leftover 1/4" ply to fit — lined with plexiglass if accidents are a possibility — or skip it for the housebroken.

Bed goes in, dog goes in, lamp goes on top. Nobody visiting will clock it as a kennel until the tail thumps.

images.alt: End table dog kennel with slatted rail sides and latched door
